speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Siegel, you were describing your home in Burgasas.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you continue, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, the children's room was on the other side of the kitchen. But it had an entrance to the kitchen. And my parents' bedroom also had an entrance to the kitchen. So we could actually walk from the children's room right through the kitchen, and into my parents room, because they were in line. And if we needed anything, you know, if we felt like waking them up, as still children do sometimes, because we had a little pain or whatever, or a nightmare, we would just run into their bedroom. But we shared that bedroom, sometimes four children at a time, because it had four beds. And the beds were up along the long walls, you know, one bed next to each other. And same on the other side. And then, up front, we had the desk and a little chest of drawers, and so forth. And also that had a door to the porch. And we could run out to the porch or the backyard. And there was a big well. We got our water from a well. And there was a huge walnut tree over the well, to shade the well. And that, we were told, was planted by my birth father before he died. And we had a lot of walnuts there, which was a lot of fun in the fall, when we would collect the walnuts. It would get our hands absolutely dark, dark green, almost black, as we shelled it, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what would your mother make?

speaker: interviewee
text: --it has an outer layer. She would make all sorts of cakes out of it. But mostly she would dry it and put away for the winter, because, you know, in the summer she baked different kinds of things with fresh fruit. But in the wintertime, she would make all sorts of things with the nuts-- you know, [? arugula, ?] and strudels, and all sorts of cakes. So the walnuts came in very handy in the wintertime. But beyond the summer kitchen, and the well, and the walnut tree, and a sandbox, was a garden where we grew vegetables, a big vegetable garden. Then a wood shed, where we kept wood for the stove, because we had a wood burning stove. And we heated the house with wood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe any of the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well Hanukkah was basically the only holiday that we celebrated in our house, because for the rest, as I mentioned earlier, we would go to my babi's in the country. But in the wintertime, we did not travel to the country. It would be-- have been too treacherous, actually. And then, of course, we would light the Hanukkah candles at home and we play with our dreidels. And we had all these candies that were made like alphabets. And we would play with it by making different words and names, and you know, it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Hebrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in a game. No, no, no. It was all Hungarian in our house. Everything was Hungarian. Which is now so strange for me that they, you know, told me I'm Ukraine. Or at least the house is in Ukraine. Because I just never thought of myself as anything but Hungarian, you know. But no, the games we played were all-- you know, with the dreidel-- we played Hebrew games. But with the alphabetic candy, no. It was all Hungarian. And then, of course, we'd light the candles. But my father, even though we had the regular candles and a menorah, liked to make the candles out of potatoes. You see, potatoes come up a second time now. He would cut like an Idaho potato in two. And he'd scoop it out, each half. And cut the bottom of the potato flat, so you could put it down, and it would be standing. And he filled them with oil and put in a wick. Homemade wick [? was ?] all from flax thread. And he liked to light those candles besides the menorah. The menorah we put in the window, but in the kitchen, we would have these candles that were really little cups of raw potatoes filled with oil and a wick. And that-- that's what we would, you know, play around.

speaker: interviewer
text: And every night he'd light another potato?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's right. Every night he would cut a fresh potato. I know. It sounds strange, but he liked doing this, because he said that was the way it used to be, you know, before people [? that ?] they had their little oil lamps, you know. But the average person who didn't have oil lamps always had potatoes. And he liked to do it that way. So he scooped it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: The other menorah you had was an-- was what? What was it made out of?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other menorah we had was brass, a brass menorah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe any of the outstanding holidays that you celebrated with your babi in-- in [? Kaniate ?]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all the holidays were really outstanding with my babi, because she really went into all this preparation. And then my mother arrived, and she joined in, and my older sisters and all. So there was more cooking and baking and doings on-- you know, going on. Oh, I remember one that was kind of unique, because it was Yom Kippur that I was there. And of course, you're not supposed to eat on Yom Kippur. And I was going to fast all day, but my babi said to me, oh you're young. You don't have to fast all day.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she said, you come with me to the temple. But we take a little bundle of food for you, because I know by noon you will be hungry. And that's-- that'll be enough fasting for you, half a day. So she made this little thing out of a cloth napkin. She tied it, like a little hobo thing, and she put some little pastries in there. And she went off around 12:00, 1 o'clock, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I asked her if I could go out, because frankly, to sit in the temple, I couldn't understand a word, you know. It was all in Hebrew. And I sat with mostly these old ladies-- because we had our own whole pew there that belonged our family, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So but at that particular time, for some reason, my parents weren't there. And she said, OK, I could go out. So I went out and I played with the other children. And they all looked at me as if I was crazy when I opened this little thing, because they were all fasting. So I quickly tied it up and decided, you know, that I was going to fast too and not eat it. And they were very approving of that, because at first they were just walking away from me, because I was eating, you know, and they were-- as I said, much more religious in the country. But babi knew, I guess, that we didn't do these things so much at home, so she wanted to go along with my mother's way.

speaker: interviewee
text: But mostly, I think I just loved the children there because I learned so much from them. They were so different than the children in the city. And they had a very different culture, especially the non-Jewish children, but even the Jewish children. And people were much more down to earth, very modest. Of course they had very little to be boasting about. And I just liked the simplicity of the life there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you maintain your friendships from year to year when you visited your grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. Absolutely. And we-- I went into their house and ate, you know, and which you're not supposed to do either, because their house wasn't kosher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family keep the Sabbath in Burgasas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. My mother lit the candles every Friday. I did the same. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she prepare the challah?

speaker: interviewee
text: A challah? Oh, yes. Oh, she'd not only break the challah, she'd baked seven loaves of bread every Friday, a loaf for each day. And we had a big mortar oven inside-- I mean outside-- in the backyard. You see, we really had a huge, long backyard, because we had so many things in it. This mortar oven was just beyond the well. And she would get up at 5 o'clock in the morning, or earlier, every Friday. And she'd go in and start-- I mean out-- and start the fire in the oven to heat the oven up. But the preparation of bread actually started the night before. On Thursday night she would get all her ingredients together. And something very special about her bread baking, because she had this little piece of dough that she kept in a little tin metal box. And every Thursday night, she took out that little tin metal box, and after she assembled her flour, and water, and all the things for her baking-- and she had this wooden thing that she did the baking in, like a wooden bowl, but that was sort of oblong. And she put the flour, and water, and everything, just the right amount. And then she would take this little ball of dough and she'd crumble it over the top and work it really through the dough, you know, and knead it, and all that. And, of course, we asked her what this special thing was, you know, about the dough. And mother said, I had brought this little ball of dough with me. When I got married, my mother gave it to me. And this way-- she got her ball of dough, my grandmother did, from her mother, my great grandmother. And she said, and this way the bread we bake is always the same from generation to generation. And every Friday, as she shaped her seven loaves of bread, she would take a little piece again, oh, the size of maybe a tennis ball. And she would put it in this little tin box for next Friday. But one of my saddest memories concerning this bread is the last Friday before they took us to the ghetto. Remembering the story so well, I stood by my mother. And I was watching to see what she was going to do, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And sure enough, she scooped up every morsel of that dough from her wooden kneading bowl, and shaped it into the last loaf of bread, the seventh loaf of bread. And I said, aren't you going to leave a little ball of dough for next Friday? And she said, there is no next Friday.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remembered how I used to ask her, you know, when I get married, will you give me a little ball of dough? And she said, of course she would. And now I realize that not only she wouldn't give me a ball of dough, but she isn't saving any even for herself. So that was a very, very emotional thing for me, because she like went back on her promise. And I knew that it wasn't, you know, her doing, that our life has changed. And what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was in the spring, just before Passover of 1944. And then, what she did with that bread was also very different than what she had done before. I came in from playing, and I found her slicing all these loaves of bread. And she put them on the top of the stove, because as I said, we had a wood burning stove that had a big black plate, huge stove, I mean. It was really like a big table.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she had all the sliced bread on the top of the stove. And she was toasting it, kept turning it over, you know? And cooling it off and putting it in a pillow case. And I said, mother, what are you doing with all this bread? Why are you toasting all this bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, we'll take it with us when the time comes. You can't take bread, she said. It won't keep. It will mildew. But if we toast it, it will keep. And at least we'll have bread. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that really was the beginning of the end, I guess. Because that very week, they took us to the ghetto. And I have a photo from this man who went back and took a photo of my house. He also took a photo of the ghetto, which was a brick factory in our town, at the end of our town. As a matter of fact, our street lead right into the brick factory. It was a straight road. And my name-- the name of my street is called [? Yarutza ?], which means factory street, because it lead-- lead right into the factory. And they had taken all the Jewish people from the little villages earlier, so my mother had already all this, not just premonition, but she had proof. Because the people had come through our streets for days, that they took from the little villages. And they were bringing them into the ghetto in our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did they look like? Death. They were just unbelievable to look at. They were so, so depressed looking. Well, you can imagine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody resist or run away?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some people that hid out. Resist? I heard my babi resisted. And I'm sure there were some other people who resisted. It was very difficult to resist when they walk into your yard with rifles. How can you resist? I'll describe to you what happened when they came to our backyard. And I'll try not to get too emotional, but-- it was so-- my mother had her toast all prepared. And these people had been going through our streets. And we've been emptying, literally emptying the well. You could see the markings on the well as we were each day coming lower and lower. And we were bringing old buckets of water then handing them to these people that were coming from the villages, you know. I mean, they would knock on your door at anytime of the night, you know. Give you five minutes to get out, not even enough time to take your medication, that you really needed, or pack a few things, or anything. But for us, we were even hoping that maybe they wouldn't take us, since we were so near the ghetto, that maybe the ghetto would fill up with all these people coming from out of town, and maybe they would just make part of our street the ghetto, you know. Move in some more Jews, or whatever. But on a Saturday, when I was actually at my neighbor's house, and mother was home alone with the two children, on the Sabbath, they invaded our yard. These Hungarian soldiers came with rifles. And they wanted us-- you know, told my mother to get ready, that she was going to be taken to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hungarians with rifles, or the SA, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungarians with rifles, because you know, the Hungarians were collaborators of the Germans. I mean, they fought on the same side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they wearing uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes. They were policemen. Policemen. Hungarian policemen with rifles came in. And my mother was screaming my name.

speaker: interviewer
text: She didn't know where you were.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came running from the neighbors. And there she was, white as a ghost, with the two children clinging next to her, my little brother, Sandorn, who as I said was 6, and my little sister Joli, 4, just scared, shaking, you know. And the policeman proceeded, one of them, to read the census, which we all Jews had to post the census outside the house. So this was on the beam of our porch.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, where is Lilli? My mother said, Lilli was taken to Poland two years ago with her husband and child. They had taken them earlier, because her husband was a political prisoner. Was that a lie?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They turned him in. He was in the army. And they turned him in that he had something bad to say about the Hungarians. So they brought him home in handcuffs. And they got his wife and his 4-year-old little girl. And they took them off to Poland. And that really was the time that my mother changed forever. She used to be so happy and gay, and entertain us all the time. Like, she loved the Jewish theater. She would go to the Jewish theater, come home and re-act the whole play for us, you know. And she was just such a gay person. On the winter times, in the mornings, I remember when the house was very cold, before the fire warmed up the house and we had to get up for school, she would tell us, well, of course, you're standing shivering. Of course it's cold. She says, hop around. She says, look. And she would, you know, take her clothes and just hop and, you know, jump around and make us laugh, and make us our breakfast. And always so full of life. But after they took away my sister and her first and only grandchild, she changed completely.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your other three sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: So they start reading the names and they said, Roszi. So my mother said, she doesn't live here. She lives with my mother in the village. The address, they wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about Etu? She's in Budapest. She-- she couldn't come home because Jews couldn't travel. And she was stuck there. Her address.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where is [? Imboya? ?] This is Violet now. And my mother said, she's someplace in town. Because what these girls had done, and boys, all the young people around 15, 16, had gotten together. And they went from house to house begging for sheets, pillowcases, a change of clothes, medication, whatever they could bring, blankets, to the ghetto for the people who were already there from the villages, and who didn't have any time to get anything for themselves. So she was on this mission. There was a horse that they had, with a little wagon, that they had allowed one of the Jews, because he was a cripple from the First World War. He lost an arm. Mr Schwartz, they had allowed him to have his little wagon with one horse. So these kids took this wagon with the horse and they would pile it up with whatever they could get, and bring it to the ghetto. So they told me to go look for her. And I had to leave my mother with the two little ones scared to death with these guys with their rifles. But mother insisted, you go. So I went, and I find my sister on Main Street. I find really just the horse and wagon first. But I knew she'd be, you know, in one of the houses. So I found her. And oh, I was hysterical crying. And what really got me, as I-- as I ran through this street, that there was nobody on the street. This was a town that usually, you know, was very populated, people going back and forth, shopping, or just cruising, you know, walking, going someplace. There was nobody on the street. And nobody was looking out their window. They knew what was going on. And they just hid in their houses, the Christians.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was exactly April of 1944. And it was just before Passover.

speaker: interviewer
text: Prior-- prior to this invasion of the Germans and the Hungarians, armed-- the armed policemen, what-- what was your prior awareness to the outbreak and progress of World War II?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, as a child, I picked up quite a bit of it, even in my grandma's village when I was only about 10, 11 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: From what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because don't forget, but they were already in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you learn from the newspapers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Newspapers. Newspapers. I wasn't reading the newspapers at the time, because I was too young. But my sister Roszi had a Hungarian policeman friend who would bring her the Hungarian newspaper. They were actually in love, those two.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you then? What-- what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess that must have been like 1940-- 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were 10 years old?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 10 years old, and this Hungarian policeman would bring her newspapers. And also, my grandmother talked my sister into-- since he's such a good friend of yours, I want you to ask him to mail some letters for us, so your parents will know that we are all right. Because there was no mail going through, you know. But he could mail it from another town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you also have a radio in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh they confiscated radios, bicycles, that was gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: When? When was that-- when did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was confiscated in 1942, when my father was taken away in a general mobilization. So actually, by the time all this trouble started, my mother was home alone with five children. She was just by herself trying to cope with five children to feed, no food, everything was rationed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at 10 years of age you had heard or read the newspaper?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: About the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I heard my grandmother and my older sister discuss what was in the newspaper-- about Poland, and the Jews, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And by 12 your radio was confiscated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you afraid? Were you frightened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, was I frightened? Not for myself. Honestly, I'm the kind of person that I'm not scared for myself, even when I was a child. I was frightened of what was going to happen to my papi, who was old, and my mother, and my father, who was in the army, especially when I saw all those bodies in the river, when there was the fight between the Hungarian and Ukraine. And I saw these dead bodies in the river.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1939. And I thought my father, who was always in the army, because he was a officer in the army. And he was always back and forth. And I've seen him a lot in uniform. And these bodies wore uniforms, Ukraine uniforms.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought, you know what will happen to my father? And then I worried about my little brother, who was going to grow up to be a soldier. So really, I wasn't that frightened for myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Hungarians came into town, I mean to the little village, everybody hid again there. It even hid the animals. Because they were very rowdy as they came in, and because of all of the gunfire and everything, before they marched in. And I ran out, I remember, from grandma's house, and tied my scarf on the gates. Because I had a knitted scarf that was red, white, and green, which is the Hungarian colors. I welcomed them, because I lived with Hungarians all my life. And to me that was nice, that now they're coming here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you? 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: 9, 9 years old. I mean, I was just a child, and I thought this be, you know, fun.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hungarians, yeah. Till I saw those bodies the next morning. Then I didn't think it was any fun. The next morning, I ran out to see if the village had changed in any way, because it's now Hungarian, you know. And I ran. And there were crocuses, I remember. It was early spring and there were crocuses growing. And I was running and picking crocuses. And when I got to the river, which was not too far from my grandmother's house, I saw these bodies of young men in the river. And that really shocked me, you know. And they looked like 18, 19 years old, just really very young. So that was you know, I guess my first introduction to war, was when I was 9 years old. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: So by the time they were rounding you up for the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the time they were rounding us up and I had seen these herds of people coming through our streets. And some were on wagons. The very old and sick, and pregnant women, and little infants, they allowed on these wagons. But the other ones were walking you know, behind the wagons. And they all cried to us, you know, as we came out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pretty lady, could we get some water? I mean, I was just a child. But trying to really, you know, speak nice to me to get some water. So we were running out with these buckets, handing up water to everybody, you know, first to the old, the children running after the wagons, and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother cut up every sheet she owns, because these women didn't have diapers for their babies, and they kept pleading for diapers. So she was cutting up whatever she could, table cloths, everything. And we were running out to bring this to these people you know, who were with babies and no way to change them.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the spring of 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And prior to this you were discussing looking for your sister and finding her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr Schwartz's wagon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr Schwartz's wagon. And I found her with her friends, some young boys and girls. They were standing get around while I was telling her what happens, you know, crying, smoking cigarettes. I remember these young people were all smoking cigarettes. They were, I guess, so nervous.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go with your sister when you found her?

speaker: interviewee
text: So my sister gave me hell. She says why are you crying? You know, you just give yourself a headache. There's nothing you can do about it. You knew they'll be coming for us. And it's just our time. So we gonna go.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, but what if they take mother and the children before we get home? What will we do? She said, we know where they'll take them. We'll find them there. Why are you getting so upset? When they got there, sure enough, there was mother with her pillow cases of toast and some other bundles that she had tied up ready to take with her.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister went into the house and came out with her duffel bag. And I realized that she too had fixed up something to take with her to the ghetto. And I also realized that I didn't prepare anything. But it was too late. These police were walking through our house and picking up whatever they thought was interesting, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which sister was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just helping themselves-- this was Violet, the one that survived with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's two years older than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's two years older than I. But she was already in with this youth group. She was in with the group that was in charge of blackout, you know, when there was sirens, they would go make sure all of the windows were black. She had taken first aid in case people were injured in the bombing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by this-- she wasn't 16 yet. She-- she had her 16th birthday really in the ghetto. But she was already really into this stuff, you know. She's been back and forth to the ghetto several times before we got there. So you came home and your sister had already prepared a duffel bag. And you noticed that your mother was standing there with the--

speaker: interviewee
text: With the stuff. And then the two little ones-- [DRINKS] as we were told to actually move to get going-- ran off to the sandbox to gather their toys, scared as anything. But they went and grabbed their little pails and shovels and stuff from the sandbox. And ran back to mother, scared that they would be, you know, punished for what they've done. And as we reached the gate, mother opened her purse. And she wanted to lock the gate door. And they said, no need for that. Everything will be boarded down.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they just marched us through the gate. And I remember my mother turning and looking back at the house. And one of the policeman shoved her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, take a good look, lady, because you'll never see it again. And I thought this was totally unnecessary to be that cruel. But that's what he said to her. And sure enough, she never did. I never did either. Never went back. But as I said, I have this photograph from this man from Virginia, who is a professor there, and invited me to come and speak at his school.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you marched with your mother, with--

speaker: interviewee
text: So we actually got on a wagon. They had a wagon waiting for us. And we got on a wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: To go where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the ghetto, the brick factory. And where they had put us, actually, is they had these open barracks with just tar roofs-- no walls at all. And mind you, this is the early spring, April, when we had all the showers and winds. And that's where they were taking us, With no walls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.
